one_line_takeaway: Empirically, ensemble models (RF, GBM) are consistently more resilient than linear/DNN models (LR, DNN) to label-flipping, outlier-injection, feature-impersonation, and synthetic-outlier poisoning across three IoT NIDS datasets (CICIoT2023, Edge-IIoTset, N-BaIoT), with label flipping causing up to 40% accuracy loss in the worst cases -- but no defense is proposed or tested.
key_finding: Clean-data baselines (Section 4.2): on CICIoT2023, RF reached 99.29% accuracy / 74.24% F1, GBM 99.06% accuracy / 71.06% F1, LR only 83.88% accuracy / 53.89% F1. On Edge-IIoTset, RF, GBM, and DNN all scored near/above 99% on accuracy, precision, and recall. On N-BaIoT, GBM reached 99.96% accuracy / 99.91% F1, RF reached 91.54% accuracy / 88.20% F1, LR reached 90.30% accuracy / 85.35% F1. Under poisoning: Label Flipping was the most damaging attack across all three datasets, with accuracy dropping by up to 40% in severe cases, and LR and DNN suffering the largest declines while RF and GBM were comparatively more stable (though still degraded). Outlier Injection produced dataset-dependent effects -- RF/GBM stayed high on Edge-IIoTset and N-BaIoT, while LR and DNN suffered sharp precision/recall loss specifically on CICIoT2023, which the paper identifies as the most vulnerable dataset to this attack. Feature Impersonation similarly hurt LR/DNN mainly on CICIoT2023, with minimal disruption on Edge-IIoTset/N-BaIoT, indicating attack effectiveness is dataset/feature-dimensionality dependent. Generic Synthetic Outliers had the mildest effect of the four attacks, causing only moderate LR degradation on CICIoT and slight decline on N-BaIoT, with GBM/RF/DNN largely stable. Overall ranking: RF and GBM consistently proved most robust across all four attack types and all three datasets; LR and DNN were consistently the most fragile, and no model showed universal resistance to all attack types.
core_contribution: An empirical robustness study (KNUST, Ghana) that retrains four supervised ML classifiers -- Random Forest (RF), Gradient Boosting Machine (GBM), Logistic Regression (LR), and a Deep Neural Network (DNN) -- on clean vs. poisoned versions of three real-world IoT intrusion-detection datasets (CICIoT2023, Edge-IIoTset, N-BaIoT), applying four poisoning techniques (Label Flipping, Outlier Injection, Feature Impersonation, Generic Synthetic Outliers) and comparing accuracy/precision/recall/F1 before and after poisoning. The paper frames this as 'the first unified robustness evaluation of four poisoning techniques across three IoT datasets using multiple ML classifiers' (Table 1, 'Our Work' row). No new attack, defense, or model is proposed; the contribution is the systematic empirical comparison itself plus qualitative recommendations for future defenses (adversarial training, anomaly-based filtering, feature-level validation).
threat_model: Training-time data-poisoning attacker who can inject or modify samples in the training set of an ML-based Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS) for IoT. Four poisoning strategies are evaluated as the paper's own manipulation methods (Section 3.4, Table 2): (1) Label Flipping -- reassigns ground-truth labels to incorrect classes; (2) Outlier Injection -- injects extreme/noisy values to distort feature distributions; (3) Feature Impersonation -- alters feature values so malicious samples imitate benign traffic; (4) Generic Synthetic Outliers -- introduces fabricated, statistically abnormal samples. The paper does not specify a poisoning budget/contamination rate, nor an attacker capability model (white-box vs black-box, centralized vs federated); federated-learning and clean-label poisoning are discussed only as related-work context (citations [17-21]), not evaluated in this study.
limitations: (1) No poisoning-rate/contamination-level ablation is reported -- 'severe cases' and percentage drops are described qualitatively/graphically rather than tabulated with exact contamination fractions in the paper. (2) N-BaIoT instance count is internally inconsistent between the Introduction ('more than seven million time-series samples') and Table 3 (1,018,298 instances), which the review does not attempt to resolve. (3) Results for the poisoned conditions are reported primarily via figures (Figs. 7-10) referenced qualitatively in text rather than as exact numeric tables in the extracted body text, so precise per-model/per-attack percentage-point deltas beyond 'up to 40%' for label flipping are not independently verifiable from the text alone. (4) No proposed or tested defense/mitigation is implemented -- recommendations (adversarial training, differential privacy, blockchain-based validation, federated + ensemble hybrids) are stated as future work, not evaluated. (5) Single hardware/software configuration, no cross-validation or statistical significance testing mentioned. (6) Threat model omits attacker capability constraints (poisoning rate, access level, white/black-box) making real-world severity hard to calibrate.

datasets_used: CICIoT2023 (1,048,575 instances, 47 features, single categorical label)
Edge-IIoTset (2,219,201 instances, 63 variables, 4 categorical features, 'Attack label' target)
N-BaIoT (1,018,298 instances per Table 3; text elsewhere describes 'more than seven million time-series samples' -- inconsistency in the source paper)
quantitative_results: CICIoT2023 clean baseline: RF 99.29% accuracy / 74.24% F1; GBM 99.06% accuracy / 71.06% F1; LR 83.88% accuracy / 53.89% F1
Edge-IIoTset clean baseline: DNN, RF, GBM all near/above 99% accuracy, precision, and recall
N-BaIoT clean baseline: GBM 99.96% accuracy / 99.91% F1; RF 91.54% accuracy / 88.20% F1; LR 90.30% accuracy / 85.35% F1
Label Flipping poisoning: accuracy dropped by up to 40% in severe cases, with LR and DNN experiencing the largest declines; RF and GBM comparatively more stable
Outlier Injection: LR and DNN suffered significant precision/recall degradation specifically on CICIoT2023; RF/GBM preserved high performance on Edge-IIoTset and N-BaIoT
Feature Impersonation: sharp accuracy/recall/precision/F1 reductions for LR and DNN on CICIoT2023; minimal disruption on Edge-IIoTset and N-BaIoT
Generic Synthetic Outliers: moderate LR degradation on CICIoT, slight decline on N-BaIoT; GBM, RF, DNN largely stable across datasets
